name: [TEST FIXTURE] Care for Children
sourceAttribution: AUTO-GENERATED TEST FIXTURE — placeholder content, not for production. Real authoring will replace this with cited content.

guides.title: Fevers by age
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder. Always consult a pediatrician for sick children, especially infants.]

Under 3 months: any rectal temperature 100.4°F (38°C) or higher is a medical emergency. Go to the ER.

3-6 months: 101°F (38.3°C) or higher — call your pediatrician.

6 months and older: a fever alone isn't usually dangerous; look at how the child is acting. Lethargy, refusal to drink, stiff neck, severe headache, or rash with fever are red flags that need immediate medical attention.

guides.title: Signs of dehydration
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Mild to moderate: dry lips, fewer wet diapers (less than 1 every 6-8 hours for infants), no tears when crying, sunken soft spot on the head (infants), lethargy.

Severe: very dry mouth, no urination for 8+ hours, sunken eyes, cold or blotchy hands and feet, extreme sleepiness or unresponsiveness. Severe dehydration is a medical emergency. For mild cases, offer small frequent sips of an oral rehydration solution; don't give plain water alone to infants under 6 months.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Infant CPR — cross-reference
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Full technique is in the First Aid pack's pediatric CPR guide. Key points for infants under 1 year:

Two fingers in the center of the chest, just below the nipple line. Compress to about 1.5 inches (one third of chest depth). Rate of 100-120 per minute. If alone: 30 compressions to 2 breaths. With a second rescuer: 15 compressions to 2 breaths.

Breaths: cover both nose and mouth with your mouth, gentle puffs only — adult-size breaths can damage tiny lungs.

guides.title: Common childhood rashes — when to worry
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Most rashes are minor (heat rash, eczema, viral exanthems). Worry when a rash appears with fever, doesn't fade when pressed with a glass tumbler (could be meningitis), spreads rapidly, includes blisters or open sores, or appears around the eyes/mouth with breathing trouble (allergic reaction).

For minor rashes: keep the skin clean and dry, avoid known irritants, use plain moisturizer. Call your pediatrician if it spreads, worsens, or is accompanied by other symptoms.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: ER vs urgent care vs pediatrician
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

ER: trouble breathing, severe allergic reaction, head injury with loss of consciousness or vomiting, suspected broken bones with deformity, severe burns, severe abdominal pain, seizures, infants under 3 months with fever, any change in mental status.

Urgent care: high fever responding to medication, minor sprains, mild cuts needing stitches, ear infections, rashes without fever.

Pediatrician (next day or call): ongoing cold symptoms, mild fever, school physicals, medication questions, growth concerns.
